speaker: interviewer
text: 1996, I'm conducting an interview with Rena Zabielak. We're in London, England. And this is tape number two. You were saying that your father was taken outside the ghetto to his shop.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was taken with my two uncles. They took out the goods to a shop of a Pole. And later, the Pole put it on a lorry, and I don't know how they sent it into the ghetto. And Daddy start-- got a business in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people still had a little bit of money?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my father was a very wealthy man. And he tried still to deal and to work. And what it was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when we were-- you know-- how shall I explain? Then it was-- you know, we had the shop. And through the ghetto they made-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] The ghetto was in two parts-- little ghetto and big ghetto. And there was a bridge, which was joining one part of the ghetto to the other part of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the bridge go  over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over a street. And our shop was on the other part of the ghetto. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not where we lived. And you had to walk about a half an hour to get from the shop to the flat. And Daddy, had in the business, he was selling all this stuff but for bakers and chocolate manufacturers. And he bought a ton of little sweets. Like Smarties, they were. And at the time, people were-- already there was no food. It was a lot of hunger. And people were coming to the shop. And they start giving three sweets to every poor person who walked in. But then they gave two. Then they gave one. And my father, whenever he went home, he used to take a pocket full of sweets. And whenever he came home, he said, this is-- I didn't have enough. Every day he was aggravated, because children were lying on the street hungry. And he gave them a sweet. And this sweet kept them alive. But one day, in that block of flats where we live was a lot of people, some of which were richer and some of which were very poor, which were starving from hunger. Then my father called all the people from the block of flats to our flat. And he spoke to them, and he said, look here. We've got to help our poor brothers and sisters. And he decided-- they decided to make a kitchen to feed everybody-- all those poor people, that they should get food every day, because otherwise they will die from starvation. And not only that say didn't have food, but it was typhus also in the ghetto. People were dying from typhus. And then the formed another-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they form in the ghetto, like, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for sick people-- I don't know how to say it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not a hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a hospital-- you see, they form that if somebody's ill and got typhus, they put him in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Isolation.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to Isolation. And in case in a block of flats somebody had typhus, they used to close up the block of flats, go in to the flats like to disinfect them. But they actually didn't disinfect. They wanted money, or they used to see what somebody got there that they could blackmail. And a lot of blackmail was going on in the ghetto. If somebody was rich, then they used to blackmail him in a different. And my father was blackmailed very, very often.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember the circumstances of the different types of blackmail?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember. For instance, when they came, and they found something, let's say, for instance, chocolate-- you couldn't manufacture chocolate-- then if they found that was manufactured chocolate, then say said, come on. Give us money. And this very often was happening. But sometimes for nothing, they used to put him to prison, and then come to the family, that the family should pay out. One day, my father had a friend who used to come every day to the shop. And this friend-- people used to-- because first of all, you couldn't go out in the evenings, because in the evenings it was closed up. After 7:00 or 8 o'clock, you couldn't go out in the evening. Then people became very friendly in the blocks of flats. And say used to help each other. They used to play cards, or had all different kinds of discussions, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] father had also a friend who was in the police. And one day, he came, and he said, you know what? You've got a friend who comes to you every day, and he said if they would put you to prison, then they can get out a lot of money from you. Then he said, don't go to the shop for a few days. And let them pretend that you are in prison, and you will see what this man will do. It was like this. My father was at home playing cards. And this man came to the shop. And my uncle told him a tragedy happened. [NON-ENGLISH] was arrested. He said, leave it to me. I will help you. He came back after an hour, and he said, you've got to give me million zlotys, and we let him free. My uncle said, million zlotys? I haven't got even this amount of money. What do you want? What shall I tell you? He was coming like this for a whole week. On the end, it came to 5,000 zlotys. And my uncle said, I'm sorry. We haven't got. And this man disappeared. He never came anymore. But then we had another incident. That block of flats they locked up because somebody had typhus, and they came to our flat to look for something. My grandmother was sitting. Then they start looking at her. What has she got there? And I got upset. An old lady-- why do they-- what they want from her? Then I said, you're not going to touch her. She's my grandmother, and I don't want you to do nothing to her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they got very annoyed with me, and they said, we will take you, and we will cut your hair away. You will now go to the [NON-ENGLISH], and there you will cut your hair. I got scared. And I went through the chimney out. And on about three inches I went from one chimney to another chimney to another house. And I found that poor girl who looked my age. And I told her that she should pretend that she's Rena [NON-ENGLISH]. And because she had her hair cut already, that she should go there and bring me the certificate. And with this certificate, I could go back to the, you know, the house. Then a cousin of mine, he got typhus. And he lived with us. And the same story happened. They sent a doctor. We had a friend, a doctor. And this doctor said, you've got to take blood from a healthy person and pretend that he got pneumonia. We took blood from somebody healthy, had it tested, and next to his bed, we put this [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] certificate. And funny enough, the doctor came and looked at the certificate and walked out.

speaker: interviewer
text: You put on a certificate he had pneumonia.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was that he hasn't got typhus screening. A few days later, came-- someone rung the bell. It was a nurse. She came to take blood from my cousin. I said, what does this doctor want? She said this doctor wants money. I said, you go and tell him that he won't get money and forget it. And you won't take blood from him. And she went, and a few days later, this doctor came-- another doctor and this woman. And the doctor had to hold my cousin's arm. And he said, come on. We will take blood. And I walked in, and I said, you won't take blood, because this woman told me that you want money. He got embarrassed. They walked, all three, away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Funny enough, later I met the same woman, because she was a laboratory assistant. And she-- I met her while I was in hiding. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Incidents like this happened all the time in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: But people were dying from hunger. And this was till [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] June, July, when we've got Tisha B'av Funny enough, that the Germans-- all the trouble when they put out the Jews in the Warsaw ghetto-- came on a Jewish holiday. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on that day, they start sending people away from work. But before this happened, they start sending young men away. And nobody heard from them. And I had an uncle who was a young man. And my father was scared that they should catch him. And he had a wife and two children, and that they would take him for such work and later you don't hear from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then came a Jewish policeman into the ghetto. I was in the ghetto. And he came to my father. He said, you know what? We could employ him as a policeman. But he doesn't have to work. He would only come every morning to report-- only report.

speaker: interviewee
text: That particular day, when the Germans start sending people out in big groups, my uncle went to the police as usual. And the chief of police said, today you've got to deliver me 500 people. And if you won't deliver-- 5,000 people. And if you won't deliver 5,000 people, we will take some of your family. He came to my father, and he told him this. And my father said, by me, you won't catch any people any more. You are going to be-- I will pay money, and we all will be employed in a factory, you know, because the Germans needed to male-- to repair their uniforms. And you will be with us repairing happening the uniforms. And that's what it was. We went. We decided to work for the Germans to repair uniforms. But this cost also money, to get in into the factories, because they didn't want just anybody. They wanted to make money out of it. And we went, we start working in the factories, and when we start working in the factories, the ghetto was in two parts. And this factory where we work was on the other part of the ghetto. And as it was on the other part of the ghetto, they were closing this part where we were. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Food was scarce at the time. You couldn't get any food. And people used to communicate, those which could, on rickshaws. You had like a rickshaw, and a man used to sit in the back, and you sit in the front. And it was like a little trolley, it was like a bicycle-- push. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] They decided that everybody has to go to the other part of Warsaw. And is it was so scared of food, we had to [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] get, somehow, food. And we had to leave our flat. A Pole came, and he said he would like to take all our furniture. And for all the furniture, he gave us two kilos of bread and a half a kilo of butter, because we had to leave the flat. But we had a lot of china, crockery, and crystal, and it was all packed in a crate. And my father said, this Pole won't take it. And I don't want the Germans to have it. And we lived on the third floor. He threw it all down the stairs, that the German shouldn't get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it broke?

speaker: interviewee
text: It broke. And we had to go back-- go to the other part of Warsaw. And at the time, already, my father's two sisters and a child went to [NON-ENGLISH] because we had some family there. And another sister of my father-- he had three sisters. And another sister of my father's, with a husband and child, went to another town. Then in the ghetto was only left my parents, myself-- my brother worked in a different factory-- and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my youngest aunt's husband and child. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when we work there-- and grandmother, we gave to a hospital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One day, before grandma went to the hospital, I went to see her to say goodbye.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she living in the same apartment as you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was somewhere else, because there she could hide. She couldn't hide in our apartment. When I went there, I said goodbye to Grandma. And when I left, going back to our apartment, I saw Dr. Janusz Korczak walking with the children to the Umschlagplatz. And this was a scene that I will never forget. He had four children in his hands-- two in each hand and two under the arms-- six children. And the rest follow him. It was something unbelievable. I can tell you forever I remind myself. Today, it breaks my  heart how this Dr. Korczak [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went with those children to the Umschlagplatz.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, did you know what was going to happen to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We still didn't know. A few days later, my mother-- no that was before-- my mother and my auntie were called and taken to the Umschlagplatz. And we somehow knew a policeman who could bring them back for money. And in the morning, they were coming back, and we went to meet them. And I passed by, on the street to go there, and the streets were full of dead bodies, because if they didn't catch you, and they saw you in the distance, they shot. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You know, I didn't tell you exactly even from before how there was a German who we called Frankenstein. He never had a cup of coffee till he killed five Jews every morning. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You see, you can't realize how hard was the life in the Warsaw ghetto. And my [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] uncle worked in the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] also in that factory. And one day, those directors from the factory wanted to make a concert. And they made them take a piano from one place to the other. Then when they took the piano, they broke the piano on the way. Then the fellow who was supposed to come to play, he couldn't play. And this [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] was the beginning of the underground in our place, because my father was one of the leaders in the underground. But there was only five of each of the leaders in the underground. And they used to get in sometimes in the middle of the night to the German director's office to listen to the news from England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then we had some news. But they were scared. In case somebody would catch them, they would be shot.

speaker: interviewee
text: You said you went to say goodbye to your grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, that's what I told you. When I went to say goodbye, and on the way back I saw Janusz Korczak.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, you went to say goodbye to your grandmother, because?

speaker: interviewer
text: They were taking her to the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, so you didn't know at that point whether you-- did it occur to you that you might not see her again?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but as she was in the hospital, I used to go every few days with a basket of food to take to my grandmother. I couldn't give it to my grandmother, and it wasn't so easy to go to take the food, because I had to go from one house to the other to hide. In case the Germans would catch me, I would be shot. And I used to give the food to the caretaker there. And they said they delivered to her. If they delivered or not, I don't know, because I didn't-- we didn't hear from grandma. But I risk every time when I went there my life going to the factory. What was happening to your brother at this time, because I know he was a little bit younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was working in a metal factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did he come back to you at night time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because my aunt-- where he worked, from my mother's side-- because my mother's sister lived there with her husband and daughter. And they were-- and he was going there at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have no contact with him at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't have contact much. But then the Germans start taking people on a selection.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when that started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was in September. They were taking people on a selection, and they put us out like soldiers. And whoever had a child, or who was-- looked older, they put on one side. And the other people they put on the other side.
